interview_name: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer

main_summary.summary: Audrey Nell Edwards Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer recount their roles as members of the St. Augustine Four, the teenage civil rights activists who in July 1963 sat in at a Woolworth's lunch counter in St. Augustine, Florida under the youth leadership of Dr. Robert B. Hayling and the NAACP Youth Council. Both speakers describe the local context, the daily picketing routine that began at St. Paul AME Church, and the July 18, 1963 sit-in at Woolworth's, McCrory's, and the service drug store with Willie Carl Singleton, Samuel White, Francis Floyd, Big Jimmy Jackson, and other youth. Arrested by Black deputy DJ Johnson and Sheriff L.O. Davis, they were sentenced under Judge Mathis after their mothers, supported by NAACP representative Mr. Shaw and lawyer Earl Johnson Sr., refused to sign a petition forbidding further demonstration. Hamilton and Ulmer served 77 days in the St. Johns County jail followed by 52 days isolation at the Florida Industrial School for Girls at Ocala; Singleton and White were sent to the Marianna reform school for boys. Released January 23, 1964, they returned to demonstrating with Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and the SCLC at the Monson Motor Lodge in June 1964, and Hamilton traveled with Ulmer to Jackie Robinson's home in Stamford, Connecticut. They reflect on later discovery of their FBI file, the Foot Soldiers Monument, and the ACCORD civil rights effort led by Gwendolyn Duncan, Barbara Vickers, and Henry and Katherine Twine.
key_themes: Dr. Robert B. Hayling's youth advisorship
Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and SCLC at Monson Motor Lodge
Jackie Robinson hosting in Stamford, Connecticut
parental refusal to sign juvenile non-demonstration pledge
erasure and recovery of St. Augustine civil rights memory
main_summary.historical_significance:
The St. Augustine Four sit-in of July 18, 1963 is one of the most consequential juvenile civil rights cases of the movement: four teenagers (Hamilton, Ulmer, Singleton, White) served lengthy sentences in Florida reform schools rather than sign a non-demonstration pledge, an act of conscience that catalyzed wider national attention to St. Augustine and helped set the stage for Dr. King's 1964 SCLC campaign there. Their testimony preserves the explicit chronology by which Dr. Hayling's local NAACP Youth Council laid the groundwork that Dr. King's SCLC then amplified, and corrects the historical record that often skips past the months between their January 1964 release and Dr. King's June 1964 arrival.

The interview also documents a deliberate civic-erasure pattern in St. Augustine: the speakers describe the city quietly demolishing civil rights landmarks (the Ponce de Leon site on US 1, the Monson Motor Lodge, attempted changes to the Slave Market), making oral testimony like this the principal surviving record. The later recognition through ACCORD's downtown plaques, the Foot Soldiers Monument, and Hamilton's Habitat for Humanity home as memorial-and-cemetery site for Singleton, Samuel White, and Ulmer's mother represents an ongoing institutional rebuilding led by Gwendolyn Duncan, Barbara Vickers, and Henry and Katherine Twine that the speakers explicitly credit.

chapters.title: St. Augustine Childhood and Movement Entry
chapters.summary: Hamilton describes growing up in St. Augustine with a vibrant Black business district on Washington Street, accompanying her mother to Woolworth's and McCrory's where they could buy school supplies but not drink from segregated fountains. She and other youth began gathering at St. Paul AME Church under the encouragement of Dr. Robert B. Hayling, Mr. Clyde Jenkins, Mrs. Lucille Plummer, and Mrs. Fannie Fuller Wood, who supplied them with picket signs.
related_events: NAACP Youth Council formation in St. Augustine

chapters.title: July 18, 1963 Sit-In at Woolworth's
chapters.summary: On July 18, 1963 the youth decided to escalate from picketing to a sit-in. After consulting at St. Paul AME Church and receiving money from barber and NAACP member Ernest Wells, sixteen teenagers walked downtown in pairs. Hamilton, Ulmer, Willie Carl Singleton, and Samuel White entered Woolworth's; Goldie and others split between McCrory's and the service drug store. They sat at the counter and refused to leave.

chapters.title: JoeAnn Ulmer's Path into the Movement
chapters.summary: Ulmer describes growing up on the west side of town "across the track," where Black children had no recreational activities. With her mother's permission she went to stay with her sister near St. Paul AME Church to join the youth council. She recalls Dr. Hayling's piano-accompanied singing meetings and the leadership of "Doc" and Janelle.

chapters.title: Arrest and County Jail
chapters.summary: Refused service with the words "we do not serve Niggas here," the youth were ordered out by Sheriff L.O. Davis and arrested by Black deputy DJ Johnson, whom Hamilton had known as a neighbor and schoolmate's father. Sixteen youth were taken to jail; the four girls and three boys were separated from the others. They sang in their cells expecting to be released, but were held overnight.
related_events: Arrest of the St. Augustine Four

chapters.title: Judge Mathis and the Parents' Refusal
chapters.summary: In court Judge Mathis demanded that the youths' parents sign a petition pledging no further demonstration until age 21. Twelve youth went home when parents signed; Hamilton, Ulmer, Singleton, and White stayed because their mothers refused. NAACP representative Mr. Shaw stood up to Judge Mathis vowing to appeal. Mrs. Singleton (Miss Nora), who suffered from a bad heart, fainted in court but never signed; Mrs. White (Miss Price) likewise held firm. The four were sentenced to reform school.

chapters.title: Dr. Hayling's Leadership
chapters.summary: Both speakers describe Dr. Robert B. Hayling as their youth advisor who motivated rather than restrained them, who "never left our side" and stood by the youth even when other Black leaders counseled caution. They credit him as "the inspiration and the father of St. Augustine" civil rights movement, noting his work began months before Dr. King arrived and that he sacrificed greatly for the cause.

chapters.title: Reform School: 77 Days and 52 Days Isolation
chapters.summary: Hamilton and Ulmer were held 77 days in the county jail, then 52 days in isolation at the Florida reform school for girls at Ocala (after Marianna), where they scrubbed floors on their knees until they could see their faces and prayed alone with a Bible. Singleton and White were taken in the middle of the night to the Marianna boys' reform school; both later refused to speak of what happened there and "took it to the grave." Hamilton frames the violence inflicted upon them as something the girls did not return: "the violence was afflicted on us but we did not afflict violence back."

chapters.title: Meeting Dr. King and the SCLC Campaign
chapters.summary: After release on January 23, 1964, Dr. Hayling brought the four youth to meet Dr. Martin Luther King and Ralph Abernathy at a private room before the June 1964 SCLC campaign in St. Augustine. King embraced them saying they "went beyond the call of duty." Going to jail in 1964 under SCLC (rather than 1963 under NAACP), Hamilton was personally chosen by King at St. Mary's Missionary Baptist Church to join his group to test the Monson Motor Lodge alongside Reverend Fred Shuttlesworth, Mary Peabody, and a young white woman. Owner Jimmy Brock refused service, police arrested Dr. King and the group, and a police dog memorably licked Dr. King.
keywords: I Woke Up This Morning with My Mind Stayed on Freedom

chapters.title: Jackie Robinson Hosts Them in Stamford
chapters.summary: After Hamilton's release that night, Jackie Robinson came to St. Augustine speaking at the Catholic church and embraced her in front of the crowd, flying her and Ulmer first class to his home in Stamford, Connecticut. The Robinson family hosted them, took them to museums and a sandal workshop, and Robinson's daughter conducted interviews with them for radio stations. Photographs from the visit are property of the Jackie Robinson Foundation. Hamilton remembers Robinson's young son David following her around with his horse.
related_events: Jackie Robinson's support of St. Augustine movement

chapters.title: Post-Movement Lives: College and Family
chapters.summary: After the Civil Rights Act's 1964 passage, all four enrolled in Florida Memorial College. Hamilton dropped out for lack of funds when the college relocated; Samuel White completed three or more years. White was "very smart" and "all about the brotherhood"; he died in Germany in 1995, his body shipped home by his mother. Ulmer moved to Jacksonville, married, raised four children there, and reflects that she wanted her children to have privileges she did not, including swimming pools.

chapters.title: Activist Record, FBI Surveillance, and Reflection
chapters.summary: Hamilton reflects that her activism record, while limiting some employment, was vindicated when historian David Nolan presented her with her FBI file at her Habitat for Humanity house dedication in 2008, revealing that she, Ulmer, Singleton, and White had been monitored by the FBI as 14-, 15-, and 16-year-olds. Both speakers describe being publicly acknowledged as the St. Augustine Four in 2004 when Earl Johnson Jr., son of their original lawyer Earl Johnson Sr., spoke at a commemoration alongside Mr. Dews.

chapters.title: Erasure, ACCORD, and the Foot Soldiers Monument
chapters.summary: Hamilton concludes that St. Augustine "swept the whole movement up under the rug," demolishing civil rights sites including the Ponce de Leon site on US 1 and the Monson Motor Lodge and attempting to alter the Slave Market. The ACCORD organization led by Gwendolyn Duncan, Barbara Vickers, Dr. Hayling, and former city commissioner Henry Twine with Katherine Twine placed downtown markers next to Woolworth's and led to the Foot Soldiers Monument in the plaza. Hamilton describes referring Duncan to Dr. Hayling for proper historical guidance.

loc_video.caption: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Saint Augustine, Florida, 2011 September 13
